text: Aqueous Phase Catalysis by Proton Exchanged Zeolites Although many uses of proton exchanged zeolites were proposed over the years essentially based on their Brønsted acidity, the most significant heterogeneous catalysis applications resulted from the exploitation of these materials in hydrocarbon and oxygenates transformations. Elucidating at the atomic scale the mechanism of these catalytic reactions has been one of the major challenges for several decades. Our knowledge of how the acid sites of zeolites, and the structural characteristics of their zeolitic framework interact for giving rise to the catalytic functions of proton exchanged zeolites, has strongly increased in the recent years. New concepts have thus emerged which are of general interest and the borderline between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis is more than ever discussed. The present review is focused on structural microscopic models for catalytic activity on the Brønsted acid sites of zeolites that result from combining together: i) recent dynamic kinetic studies, ii) information derived from in situ or even more often from operando 1S NMR, IR, and NMR spectroscopies and iii) quantum chemistry modeling of zeolites and of transition states involved in catalytic reactions of diverse kinds. This review first covers the state of the art relative to the Brønsted acidic sites located in zeolites for catalysis, which includes some general comments on the zeolites features playing a crucial role in heterogeneous catalysis. A particular emphasis is then given to the mechanisms investigated with higher degree of reliability on typical reactions such as those of alkenes, alkanes and aromatics, as well as on alcohols and polyhydroxy aldehydes. These reaction systems share the fact that the elucidation of the elementary steps implying C-C, C-H and/or C-O bond scissions and formations have been the most investigated and are believed to be the most understandable and generally relevant to the catalytic functions of zeolites. Some suggestions are given for future directions likely to lead to progress in our understanding of the mechanisms of the catalytic reactions occurring on zeolitic acidic sites, which should open the way to new design methods for high performance zeolite catalysts for heterogeneous Brønsted acid catalysis.
